turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Kennedy has our opinion this morning in case 16-499, Jesner versus Arab Bank.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: The petitioners in this case or deceased members of the petitioners' families were victims of terrorist attacks committed abroad. They now seek to recover damages from the respondent and the respondent is the Arab Bank PLC. Petitioners alleged that the bank allowed funds to be transferred to terrorist groups in the Middle East and these lawsuits were filed in the United States District Court for the Eastern District of New York under the Alien Tort Statute and that's commonly referred to as the ATS. The district court later ordered the suits dismissed and the Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit affirmed that Order of Dismissal. The question here is whether the judiciary has the authority in an ATS action to impose liability on a foreign corporation without any explicit authorization from Congress to do so. The court now holds that the answer is no. In Sosa v. Alvarez-Machain this court held that the ATS is jurisdictional only but that in certain narrow circumstances courts may recognize a common law cause of actions for claims based on present-day law of nations in addition to certain historical paradigms familiar when the ATS was enacted one of those examples as piracy on the high seas. The court in Sosa was quite explicit however and holding that ATS litigation can raise serious separation of powers and foreign relations concerns. Accordingly the court emphasized that federal courts must exercise great caution before recognizing new forms of liability under the ATS. Now this necessary caution is consistent with other recent precedents that cast doubt on the authority of courts to extend or create private causes of action under any circumstances and in fact the separation of powers concerns that council against courts creating private right of action apply with particular force in the context of the ATS. The political branches, not the judiciary, have the responsibility and institutional capacity, not just to determine when private parties should be able to recover damages in court but also to evaluate the resulting foreign policy consequences. Consistent with these principles it would be inappropriate for courts to extend ATS liability to foreign corporations absent further action from Congress. Even in areas less fraught with foreign policy consequences, the court looks to analogous statutes for guidance on the appropriate boundaries of judge-made cause of actions and hear the logical place to look for a statutory analogy to an ATS, common law action -- the common law action that we are asked to impose here is the best statutory analogy is the Torture Victims Protection Act, the only cause of action under the ATS created by Congress rather than the courts. The key feature of the Torture Victims Protection Act for this case is that it limits liability to individuals and the court is held that this unambiguously limits liability to natural persons. Congress' decision to exclude liability for corporations and actions brought under the TVPA is all but dispositive in the present case. Other considerations relevant to the exercise of judicial discretion also counsel against allowing liability under the ATS for foreign corporations, absent instructions from Congress to do so. In the context of criminal tribunals international law itself generally limits liability to natural persons and these are recounted in the opinion for the Court. If moreover the Court were to hold that foreign corporations may be held liable under the ATS that precedent-setting principle would imply that other nations also apply in the law of nations could hail our corporations into their courts. Now this judicially mandated doctrine in turn could subject American Corporations to an immediate constant risk of claims seeking to impose massive liability for the alleged conduct of their employees and subsidiaries around the world all as determined in foreign courts, and this often might deter the active corporate investment that contributes to the economic development that so often is an essential foundation for human rights. Of course, the corporate form can be used for sinister purposes to inflict great harm and suffering. This poses serious questions for the international community and for the Congress. The urgency and complexity of the problem may get all more important that Congress determine whether victims of human rights abuses may sue foreign corporations under the ATS. The ATS was intended to promote harmony in international relations by granting foreign plaintiffs remedy for international violations but here and in similar cases the opposite is occurring, for 13 years this litigation has caused significant diplomatic tensions with Jordan. And Jordan is a critical ally and one of the world's most sensitive regions. Jordan considers the instant litigation to be a grave affront to its sovereignty. This is not the first time that a foreign sovereign has appeared in this court to note its objections to ATS litigation. These are the very foreign relation's tensions the first Congress sought to avoid. Judicial deference requires that any imposition of corporate liability on foreign corporations for violation of international law under the ATS must be determined in the first instance by the political branches of the government. Accordingly, the court holds that foreign corporations may not be defendants in suits brought under the ATS and the judgment of the Court of Appeals is affirmed. Justice Thomas has filed a concurring opinion. Justice Alito has filed an opinion concurring in part and concurring in the judgment in which Justice Thomas has joined. Justice Gorsuch has filed an opinion concurring in part and concurring in the judgment in which Justice Thomas has joined. Justice Sotomayor has filed a dissenting opinion in which Justices Ginsburg, Breyer and Kagan have joined.
